Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty intense but kind of scattered. I'd dedicate around 26 hours a week to studying, but it wasn't always organized in the best way. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often set up practice problems or try to physically work through concepts rather than just reading. With nine online courses going on, I had to juggle a lot, and sometimes I'd just grind through whatever felt most urgent that day.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due. I tried to keep some consistency, but with so many courses and deadlines constantly shifting, I'd end up cramming more during certain weeks. When anxiety would spike, I'd sometimes study way more than planned, other times I'd feel paralyzed and barely get started. It wasn't the healthiest cycle, but I made it work somehow.
Q3: Usually it was checking if something was due or if grades were posted. I'd log in when I remembered I had an assignment coming up or when I got an email notification. Sometimes I'd go in just to see if there was anything new, but honestly, I wasn't one of those people who hung out on the LMS for fun. It was more transactional for me.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became something I naturally gravitated toward. I'd use it when I had to, but it never felt like a habit that stuck. Maybe if it had more interactive or hands-on elements, I would've engaged more regularly. The gamified tasks they introduced did make me check in a bit more though, because at least those felt like I was doing something active.
Q5: The gamified tasks were actually pretty useful because they kept me from getting too bored. I also found the practice quizzes helpful since I could test myself and actually do something rather than just passively watch. Videos were okay, but I'd often zone out unless I was taking notes or following along with something physical. Anything that let me engage actively worked better for my learning style.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion boards, honestly. Posting felt awkward and I never really saw the point when I could just figure things out myself or look stuff up. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because with limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what would actually help my grades. Some of the longer videos I'd skim through or skip entirely if they seemed repetitive.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to exams or assignments. If it was interactive or hands-on, even better. I also appreciated when things had clear outcomes, like earning points in the gamified tasks or seeing immediate feedback on practice problems. Anything that felt like busy work or didn't connect to my actual performance just made me more bored and frustrated.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like a waste of time most of the time. With my anxiety already high and so much required stuff to get through, I couldn't justify spending energy on things that wouldn't count. Required activities at least had stakes attached, so I'd push through even when I wasn't feeling it. Optional stuff just got pushed to the bottom of my list and usually never happened.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download materials and study them offline constantly because my internet isn't always reliable and I couldn't afford to waste data. I'd print things out when I could or write notes by hand to really work through the material. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen, working through problems on paper or practicing concepts physically.
Q10: There were tons of times when I was studying hard but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd download a PDF once and then spend hours going through it with handwritten notes. Or when I'd work through practice problems on paper instead of using the online tools. My exam scores show I learned the material, but my LMS activity definitely doesn't reflect all that effort.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my standard approach. I'd access something once, download it, and then work with it offline for days. It saved data and let me study without worrying about connectivity issues. The LMS would just show one login or one download, but I'd have spent way more time with that material than the system could ever track.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially when my anxiety was really bad. Sometimes I'd feel so overwhelmed by everything that I couldn't even start studying. The boredom was also a real issue, like when content felt repetitive or unstimulating, I'd just check out mentally. Financial stress and not having great resources made it harder to stay focused too.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed about money and life stuff, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. I'd do the bare minimum to get by, maybe watch a video at 2x speed or skim through readings. The cognitive load of managing nine courses meant I had to constantly triage, and sometimes the LMS engagement just got sacrificed for more urgent priorities.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about other deadlines or just feeling too burned out to absorb anything. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to mark them as complete without really processing the content. It felt hollow, but I was just trying to survive the semester.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on assignments even when I knew they mattered. The anxiety would make me avoid things that felt overwhelming, which was ironic because avoiding them just made the anxiety worse. Sometimes I'd put off discussion posts because they felt pointless, or delay starting big assignments because I didn't know where to begin. It's a cycle I'm still working on.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to consistently use the LMS. I'd check in more frequently as due dates approached and cram to get things submitted. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have logged in much at all. They created stress, but they also created structure that I needed to actually get things done.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback motivated me to keep going and showed me I was on the right track. But when feedback was slow or vague, it was frustrating and made me care less about putting effort into the next thing. Quick, specific feedback definitely helped me engage more with subsequent materials.
Q18: The structure was pretty rigid, which had pros and cons. Having clear deadlines helped me stay on track, but the lack of flexibility made it hard when life got complicated. The gamified elements they added were actually helpful because they broke up the monotony. But overall, the course design assumed everyone had consistent access and energy, which wasn't always my reality.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier because they gave me something active to do. But the heavy reliance on video content was tough for me as a kinesthetic learner. I needed more hands-on activities and fewer passive watching sessions. Also, the discussion requirements felt forced and didn't really add to my learning.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to figure things out but struggling to actually complete work. Maybe they're anxious about submitting something imperfect, or they're spending time understanding the material but can't translate that into finished assignments. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity, especially when you're stressed or confused.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I got a distinction because I was learning effectively offline. I'd study from downloaded materials, work through problems on paper, and really prepare for exams without much of that showing up in the system. The LMS only captures a fraction of what students actually do.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or how much time you spent studying offline. They miss the mental effort, the anxiety, the real learning that happens away from the screen. They're useful for tracking surface-level behavior but not for understanding actual engagement or learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, and connecting concepts rather than passively consuming content. For me, engagement is hands-on and effortful. It's not about how many times I logged in, but about whether the learning actually stuck.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety throughout the course, or how much offline studying I did with limited resources. It wouldn't capture the mental energy I spent managing nine courses simultaneously, or how the gamified tasks actually helped keep me from completely checking out. My distinction grade came from effort the system never saw.
